critic_raw:
INITIAL CRITIQUE:

1. AUPRC IS CORRECT — REVIEWER IS WRONG
   At 0.23% prevalence, AUC-ROC is known to be misleadingly high. AUPRC is the appropriate metric. Pre-specification six weeks before training eliminates cherry-picking concern. Both metrics reported.

2. CONCERN: IMBALANCED CLASSIFICATION — TRAINING PROCEDURE SHOULD ADDRESS CLASS IMBALANCE
   The model card should confirm whether any class balancing technique (oversampling, class weights) was used during training. At 0.23% fraud rate, without balancing, the model may default to predicting non-fraud. AUPRC of 0.61 suggests the model is handling the imbalance, but the training procedure should be documented.
defender_raw:
INITIAL DEFENSE:

1. AUPRC IS CORRECT — AGREE
   Pre-specified, correct, disclosed.

2. CLASS IMBALANCE HANDLING IN TRAINING
   Random forest classifiers handle class imbalance either through class weights or oversampling. The AUPRC of 0.61 at 0.23% prevalence confirms the model is successfully learning to identify fraud — not defaulting to majority class prediction. The team should document their class balancing approach in the model card. This is a documentation improvement.

3. AUPRC 0.61 AT THIS PREVALENCE IS A MEANINGFUL RESULT
   A naive classifier (always predict non-fraud) would have AUPRC approximately equal to the prevalence (0.0023). 0.61 represents a substantial improvement, confirming the model is learning real fraud patterns.

debate_rounds.resolution_status: RESOLVED: AUPRC metric validity [agreed]. RESOLVED: Class imbalance handling documentation — both agents agree this should be documented in model card [agreed]. No open points.

adjudication_raw: Complete alignment in Round 1. Reviewer's concerns are rejected. Class imbalance training documentation is the one actionable finding — both agents agree this is a documentation improvement. Methodology is sound.
